markdown:
## Population Growth Facts


1. For most of human history **population growth was negligible** due to high mortality (especially among children) despite high fertility rates. 
2. **Rapid population growth** dates back to the late 18th and 19th Centuries due to introduction of basic healthcare and improved living conditions in future HICs.

![Global Population Size, Source: Our World in Data, https://ourworldindata.org/population-growth](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/0d618f98628e8062677535b0d55c32736de66cb0-3400x2400.png)

1. The world population has reached **8 billion** and is expected to stabilize by the end of 21st Century at between 10 to 12 billion. By 2100-2150 it could be down to 9 billion.
2. In 2023 India has overtaken China as the most populated country in the world
3. About 10% of world’s population is over 65 - by 2100 this proportion will have risen to one-third
4. The **average total fertility rate dropped significantly** from 5.06 in 1964 to 3.28 in 1990 and then further to 2.3 currently.

![Total Fertility Rate Trend, Source: Our World in Data, https://ourworldindata.org/population-growth](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/41b233037911658f435ff22e22bc3451a56f11e8-3400x2943.png)

1. Now Over **50%** of the world's population can be found in **Asia, **but its share in global population will decline whereas** Africa's will increase.**
2. Population growth in Asia will be over by the mid-21st Century, whereas** Africa is the only region projected to experience further population growth throughout the 21st Century.**

![Population by World Region, Source: Our World in Data, https://ourworldindata.org/population-growth](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/7523e2b8788cb15371f0395c19de509763b8f83f-3400x2706.png)



## Trends in Fertility, Mortality, and Dependency


### Natural Increase: The Balance Between Births and Deaths


<definition term="Natural increase">**Natural increase** is the difference between the **crude birth rate** (CBR) and the **crude death rate** (CDR), expressed as a percentage.</definition>

#### Calculating Natural Increase


1. **Crude Birth Rate (CBR)**: Number of live births per 1,000 people per year.
2. **Crude Death Rate (CDR)**: Number of deaths per 1,000 people per year.

$$\text{Natural Increase (%) = CBR - CDR}$$

![Global rates of natural increase](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5de77894ecf05861c8bc4cad65bae3128f434efc-1107x633.jpg)

### Fertility Rate: The Drivers of Population Growth


<definition term="Total Fertility Rate (TFR)">The **total fertility rate (TFR)** is the average number of children a woman is expected to have during her reproductive years (ages 15–49), also called childbearing age.

The **TFR of 2.1** is called a **replacement rate** - rate that allows to maintain the same population size over time.</definition>

#### Influencing Factors


1. **Status of Women**: Higher education and employment opportunities often lead to lower fertility rates.
2. **Economic Conditions**: In LICs, children may be seen as economic assets, increasing fertility rates.
3. **Cultural and Religious Norms**: Some societies encourage larger families.
4. **Access to Healthcare**: Availability of contraception and maternal health services can reduce fertility rates.

![Large families prevail in LICs such as Nigeria](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/3d5da93b833c1647e8a4df98f0060ae04be32be2-800x518.jpg)

<callout type="example">Japan has **high female employment rates** but **low fertility** (1.2) due to the high cost of childcare and cultural work-life balance challenges.</callout>

<callout type="example">Nigeria's fertility rate remains **high** (5.2 children per woman) due to reliance on agriculture and limited access to contraception.</callout>

### Dependency Ratio: Balancing the Working and Non-Working Populations


<definition term="Dependency ratio">* The **dependency ratio** measures the proportion of dependents (people aged below 15 and above 65) to the working-age (economically active) population (15–64).
* **Older dependency ratio **measures** **the proportion of old dependents (people aged above 65) to the working-age (economically active) population (15–64).</definition>

#### Calculating Dependency Ratio


$$\text{Dependency Ratio} = \frac{\text{Number of Dependents}}{\text{Working-Age Population}} \times 100%$$

![Dependency ratios by country](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/408aa4e02404e9fd92caf00a18b317796c7f0aa6-1200x847.png)

<callout type="warning">* Don't confuse the dependency ratio with the proportion of elderly people.
* Remember, the dependency ratio includes both the young and the elderly.</callout>

<callout type="note">* While the dependency ratio is a useful indicator, it is a **crude** measure. 
* Many people work **beyond** age 64 or remain in education past age 15.</callout>

## Key Demographic Metrics


<definition term="Infant Mortality Rate (IMR)">Number of deaths of children under 1 year per 1,000 live births.</definition>

<definition term="Child Mortality Rate (CMR)">Number of deaths of children under 5 years per 1,000 live births.</definition>

<definition term="Life Expectancy">Average number of years a person is expected to live **based on current mortality rates**.</definition>

1. Life expectancy varies widely across the world.
  1. In **HICs**, it exceeds 80 years.
  2. In **LDCs**, it ranges between 50 and 55 years.

### Women Generally Live** **Longer Than Men


1. Factors include lower retirement ages, reduced physical labor, and lower participation in physically dangerous jobs (e.g. mining, construction).
2. Less direct involvement in conflict and significantly lower participation in organized crime and interpersonal violence.
3. Men are also more likely to engage in “self-destructive” and risky behaviors like excessive smoking and drinking.

<callout type="example">In some countries, life expectancy can be **declining** due to poverty, conflict (e.g. South Sudan, Syria), and diseases like AIDS (e.g. Botswana in the 1990s).</callout>

<callout type="tip">Don't assume that a longer life expectancy equates to a higher quality of life. Many elderly individuals face health challenges, poor living conditions, and social isolation.</callout>

## Population Growth Can Continue Despite Falling Fertility Rates


<definition term="Population momentum ">Population momentum refers to the **continued** population growth despite falling fertility rates below replacement rate (2.1).</definition>

1. This occurs when a** large** proportion of the population is in their **childbearing years**, leading to higher absolute numbers of births even with lower fertility rates.

## The Demographic Transition Model Describes Population Change In Stages


1. Imagine two countries: **Japan** and **Nigeria**.
  1. **Japan** faces an **aging population** with a shrinking workforce.
  2. **Nigeria** grapples with **rapid population growth** and a youthful demographic. 
2. These contrasting scenarios reflect the stages of the **Demographic Transition Model (DTM)** and illustrate how **trends** in fertility, mortality, and dependency shape societies over time.

<definition term="Demographic Transition Model (DTM)">The **demographic transition model** (DTM) is a theoretical framework that describes population change over time through distinct stages of development. It examines the relationship between birth rates, death rates, and overall population growth as a country develops economically and socially.</definition>



## DTM Shows The Transition From High Birth and Death Rates to Low Birth and Death Rates


### The Demographic Transition Model (DTM): Key Concepts


1. The **DTM** shows how populations change as countries develop, transitioning from **high birth and death rates** to **low birth and death rates** over time.
2. It highlights the relationship between economic development and demographic changes.
3. The model is based on historical data from countries like **England**, **Wales**, and **Sweden**, but not all countries follow this exact pattern.
4. Modern developing countries may progress differently, influenced by technological advancements and social policies.

![Demographic Transition Model, Source: Our World in Data, https://ourworldindata.org/demographic-transition](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/fb219f400b8a8d7448dfff3136c59176c5ae7aa6-2364x1875.png)

<callout type="note">* Modern developing countries often move through stages 2 and 3 **much faster **than European countries did historically.
* This is due to a number of reasons including** speed of technological adoption**, access to **modern healthcare**, **government policies**, and **shifting cultural attitudes**.</callout>

## There are 5 Stages to the DTM


### Stage 1: High Stationary (Pre-Industrial)


1. **High **and **fluctuating** birth and death rates
2. **Little** to **no** population growth
3. Characteristic of** pre-industrial** societies
4. **Common causes of death include**:  poor sanitation and hygiene, limited medical knowledge, food scarcity, high infant mortality, prevalence of diseases.

### Stage 2: Early Expanding


1. **Death rates** begin to **fall rapidly**
2. Birth rates **remain high**
3. Period of **rapid population growth**
4. **Falling death rates are caused by improvements in**: food supply, basic healthcare, and sanitation.

<callout type="example">Afghanistan, Niger, and Sudan are all examples of countries in the second stage of the DTM.</callout>

### Stage 3: Late Expanding


1. **Death rates** continue to **remain low**
2. Birth rates **begin to fall**
3. Population **is still growing** but at a **decreasing rate**
4. **Social changes include**: urbanization, access to education, family planning, women entering the workforce.

<callout type="example">Brazil, India and Mexico are examples of countries in the third stage of the DTM.</callout>

### Stage 4: Low Stationary


1. **Both** birth and death rates are **low**
2. Population growth **stabilizes**
3. Characteristic of **developed nations**
4. **Features include:** advanced healthcare, family planning, high cost of raising children, aging population.

<callout type="example">Most developed nations fall in the fourth stage of the DTM.</callout>

### Stage 5: Declining (Post-Industrial)


1. Birth rates **fall below** death rates
2. Aging population leads to an overall **population decline**
3. **Characterized by**: aging population, fertility below replacement rate, high dependency ratio, economic challenges.

<callout type="example">Japan is a prominent country that falls in the fifth stage of the DTM.</callout>

## Not All Countries Follow the Exact Scheme of the DTM


1. Though widespread, the DTM is based primarily on **European** experiences, where **alternative demographic transitions** may also exist. 
2. The** time frame **varies significantly between countries.
3. Modern developing countries may progress through stages **differently** than **historical **examples.

<callout type="case_study">### Ireland's Alternative Demographic Transition


1. Ireland deviates from the traditional DTM due to the devastating impact of the **1845-1852 Great Famine**.
2. The classic DTM shows death rates falling before birth rates as development progresses.
3. Instead, Ireland experienced: 
  1. A catastrophic** rise in death rates **(approximately 1 million deaths)
  2. **Massive emigration** (another million people)
  3. **Falling birth rates** due to **loss of young adults**
4. This created an **inverse pattern** where both birth rates fell and death rates rose simultaneously.
5. The demographic shock led to **persistent population decline** rather than the growth typically expected in early DTM stages.
6. Ireland's case demonstrates how **significant historical events **can create **alternative** paths through the demographic transition.</callout>

## Population Pyramids Graphically Represent Age and Sex Distribution


<definition term="Population pyramid">The population pyramid **represents the breakdown of the population by gender and age at a given point in time.**</definition>

## Key Components of Population Pyramids


1. **Vertical axis:** Age groups (typically in 5-year bands)
2. **Horizontal axis**: Population percentage or absolute numbers
3. **Left side**: Male population
4. **Right side**: Female population

![Population pyramids](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/e84ce6447834166f6749954b25621e9a20afbc72-1280x720.jpg)

<callout type="exam_technique">When analyzing pyramids, always consider both the shape **and** the implications for social and economic planning.</callout>

## There are Three Main Types of Pyramids


### Expanding (Stages 1-2 of DTM)


1. **Wide base, narrow top **represents high birth rates, high death rates (if slopes are concave), and large young dependent populations. 
2. They are characterized by a **high **fertility rates, **short** life expectancy, and **rapid **population growth.  

### Stationary (Stages 3-4 of DTM)


1. **More rectangular shape** represents similar percentages across age groups and a relatively stable population.
2. They are characterized by **moderate **birth and death rates, a **longer **life expectancy, and **slower** population growth. 

### Contracting (Stage 5 of DTM)


1. A narrow base, with a wide middle, relatively wide top represents low birth rates, low death rates, and an aging population.
2. They are characterized by having **below replacement** fertility levels, a **very long **life expectancy, and** population **decline.  

<definition term="Below replacement ">Below replacement level fertility rate refers to **a situation where the average number of children born per woman is less than 2.1**, which is the number needed to replace the existing population without considering mortality rates.</definition>

<callout type="example">**Japan**, **Germany** or **Poland**, countries in stage 5 of the DTM model, fit this description.</callout>

## Reflection and Broader Implications


<callout type="self_review">* What are the global patterns and trends of population growth?
* To what extent does population growth remain a challenge?
* What other demographic challenges, besides population growth, are significant?
* How does population momentum affect future growth even in countries with declining fertility rates?</callout>
